tool_call_sequence.description: Initiates evidence-based, lower-risk pharmacologic support when appropriate, paired with first-line behavioral treatment; requires interaction/contraindication screening before prescribing.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly using the term "insomnia" and distinguishes acute vs chronic based on duration and frequency (≥3 nights/week and ≥3 months for chronic).
Assesses key insomnia features: sleep-onset vs sleep-maintenance vs early-morning awakening, total sleep time, daytime impairment, and sleep schedule variability.

Screens for common secondary causes and mimics before finalizing the plan: depression/anxiety, substance/caffeine/alcohol use, medication contributors (e.g., stimulants, steroids), pain/nocturia, and shift work/circadian rhythm disorder.

Screens for sleep apnea and other sleep disorders when indicated (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness, restless legs symptoms) and avoids sedative-hypnotics if high suspicion until evaluated.
Explains diagnosis in patient-friendly language and includes expected course and goals of therapy (improve sleep quality and daytime function, not just hours slept).

Explains treatment and must explicitly include both "CBT-I" and "sleep hygiene" as first-line management; describes at least 2 CBT-I components (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation).

Performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about allergies (non-critical), and explicitly checks for drug interactions and contraindications (both critical) relevant to the chosen medication and patient factors (pregnancy, liver disease, concomitant sedatives, OSA risk).

If prescribing any sedative-hypnotic (e.g., zolpidem, benzodiazepines, doxepin), provides safety counseling: next-day impairment, complex sleep behaviors, fall risk, avoidance of alcohol/other CNS depressants, lowest effective dose, short duration, and reassessment plan.
Avoids inappropriate prescribing: no benzodiazepines as first-line for uncomplicated insomnia; avoids long-term hypnotic use without CBT-I and follow-up.
Provides a follow-up timeframe (typically 2-4 weeks) and clear return precautions (worsening mood/suicidality, severe daytime sleepiness, suspected apnea, adverse medication effects).

minimal_paths.description: Patient suspected of adrenal crisis in Sheehan syndrome; treat emergently then proceed with thyroid replacement only after stabilization; refer urgently.
steps: Explain concern for Sheehan syndrome with secondary adrenal insufficiency and potential adrenal crisis.
Perform contraindication check emphasizing that thyroid hormone must not precede steroids in suspected adrenal insufficiency.
Prescribe emergency-dose hydrocortisone regimen and supportive measures (documented in prescription/instructions as appropriate for setting).
Defer levothyroxine until after glucocorticoids started and patient stabilized; then initiate with free T4 monitoring plan.
Refer urgently to endocrinology (and emergency care pathway if clinically indicated).

acceptable_variants.description: Agent documents broader diagnosis wording (e.g., 'panhypopituitarism due to Sheehan syndrome') while still explicitly addressing secondary adrenal insufficiency and correct treatment sequencing.
steps: Record diagnosis as Sheehan syndrome/panhypopituitarism with secondary adrenal insufficiency.
Prescribe hydrocortisone first with stress-dose counseling.
Start levothyroxine after steroids; monitor via free T4.
Refer to endocrinology for full axis replacement planning and pituitary imaging coordination.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent includes additional hormone considerations (e.g., estrogen/progestin if appropriate, desmopressin only if diabetes insipidus suspected) without delaying life-saving glucocorticoid replacement.
steps: Record diagnosis including suspected multi-axis pituitary failure.
Prescribe hydrocortisone first; provide sick-day rules and medical alert advice.
Prescribe levothyroxine after glucocorticoids; free T4-based titration plan.
Refer to endocrinology for evaluation of gonadal axis, prolactin deficiency counseling, ± GH, and imaging.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent chooses alternative glucocorticoid replacement (e.g., prednisone equivalent) while maintaining appropriate dosing equivalence and stress-dosing education; still starts thyroid hormone only after steroid coverage.
steps: Record Sheehan syndrome with secondary adrenal insufficiency.
Prescribe an equivalent glucocorticoid regimen with clear stress-dose instructions.
Then prescribe levothyroxine with central hypothyroidism monitoring plan.
Refer to endocrinology for comprehensive management.
